text: The surgeon uses a sponge to keep the small bowel out of the surgical field and begins the medial to lateral dissection for a right colectomy.
The surgeon continues dissection into the avascular plane, carefully extending laterally while avoiding the ureter.
As the surgeon moves cephalad during the dissection, the duodenum comes into view, requiring careful navigation to avoid injury.
The surgeon isolates the iliocolic pedicles at their origin near the superior mesenteric vein, visible as a blue structure in the surgical field.
The surgeon isolates a vessel located anteriorly, using careful dissection techniques to separate it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies the vein, correcting the previous reference to its anterior position.
The junction of the isolated vessel with the superior mesenteric vein is visible, highlighted by the blue coloration of the vein.
The surgeon clears lymphatic tissue surrounding the iliocolic pedicles at their origin near the superior mesenteric vein, revealing the blue hue of the vein.
The surgeon uses a vessel sealer to divide the isolated iliocolic pedicles, which are positioned at the origin of the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon targets the artery located just deep to the previously isolated vessel, preparing to dissect or manipulate it.
The surgeon brings the artery into view, highlighting its proximity to the superior mesenteric vein.
The surgeon sweeps down the duodenum to achieve a clear circumferential view of the anatomical area, ensuring all surrounding structures are visible.
The surgeon has dissected around the ileocolic artery, preparing to seal the vessel using a vessel sealer.
The surgeon follows the medial plane, gradually exposing the pancreas as the surrounding tissue is carefully dissected.
The surgeon approaches the superior mesenteric vessels, carefully dissecting along their course to expose them within the surgical field.
The surgeon continues dissection in a cephalad direction, with the middle colic vessels beginning to come into view.
The surgeon observes a long middle colic trunk during the dissection, visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon traces the anatomical structures, revealing the origin of the middle colic artery near the superior mesenteric vessels.
The surgeon opens the peritoneum over the right branch of the middle colic vessels, revealing the bifurcation. Scissors are used for precise dissection, highlighting a vein in the surgical field.
The surgeon examines a vessel in the vicinity where the loop of Henle or the right colic vein would typically be located, attempting to identify its anatomical origin.
The surgeon continues to dissect and isolate the right branch of the middle colic vessels, which are now visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon dissects around the right branch of the middle colic vein, carefully isolating it from surrounding tissues.
The surgeon uses scissors to dissect and isolate the right branch of the middle colic vein, preparing to take the vessel.
The surgeon identifies the right branch of the middle colic artery and prepares to ligate it, with the vessel clearly visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon proceeds to dissect straight up the transverse colon mesentery towards the planned margin, following the isolation of the right branch of the middle colic vessels.

The surgeon continues dissection around the right branch of the middle colic vein, encountering marginal vessels while ensuring adequate lymphatic clearance at the root origin of previously taken vessels.
The surgeon advances dissection up to the planned margin of the transverse colon, preparing to divide the tissue at this location.

The surgeon splits the omentum on the cephalad surface of the specimen, entering the vascular plane at the junction with the colon. The omentum is taken with the specimen, and the hepatic flexure is mobilized by dissecting over the top.
The surgeon demonstrates the ease of dissection at the base section, having previously completed a medial dissection through the plane above the gastrocolic ligament.
The surgeon continues to mobilize the hepatic flexure, advancing around the flexure to further expose the transverse colon mesentery.
The surgeon continues the lateral dissection, facilitated by a prior medial dissection, easily separating the few remaining cell layers of tissue tethering laterally.
The surgeon approaches the terminal ileum (TI) and appendix from a cephalad direction, noting the challenge of maintaining this approach as they progress inferiorly.
The patient is positioned in a slight Trendelenburg, and the surgeon approaches from an inferior angle to separate the terminal ileum mesentery from its origin.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection, freeing the terminal ileum (TI) and entire colon. The small bowel mesentery is left for last, ensuring the TI is fully mobilized and unfolded. The surgeon checks the perfusion to the margins.
The surgeon performs stapling from the suprapubic port, using the port's access to secure tissue structures.
The surgeon places the specimen in the pelvis and aligns the small bowel with the colon, ensuring the mesentery is untwisted.
The surgeon performs a colotomy followed by an enterotomy, creating openings in the colon and small intestine respectively.
The surgeon adjusts the angle of their arm to ensure the stapler can be effectively inserted through the suprapubic port.
The surgeon creates an anastomosis with visible ease, using the suprapubic port approach to align and connect the bowel segments.
The surgeon sutures the common channel closed after creating the anastomosis, using precise stitching techniques to ensure closure.
The surgeon performs a running baseball stitch around the bottom corner of the surgical site, transitioning the stitch at a specific point.
The surgeon performs a baseball stitch to close the top part of the common channel enterotomy, ensuring the suture line is secure.
The surgeon cinches the suture tightly with each stitch while closing the common channel.
The surgeon carefully guides the suture down to ensure precise placement for effective closure of the enterotomy.
The surgeon imbricates the tissue, folding it back down to ensure proper alignment and closure.
The surgeon uses a three or six inch V-Loc suture for the procedure.
The surgeon enlarges the enterotomy to a reasonable size, ensuring it does not stretch or tear while positioning the stapler.

The surgeon takes a final look at the surgical site before covering it with omentum. The robotic instruments are then undocked, and the specimen is extracted through the suprapubic port using a phanaceal approach.
